records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 40 
Second, the Pañji's dual-axis exogamy (gotra + sapiṇḍa) operating 
simultaneously on patrilineal and bilateral dimensions is more sophisticated 
than any other traditional system. Chinese zúpǔ enforces surname exogamy 
(single axis, patrilineal). Islamic nasab tracks patrilineal descent for 
inheritance without systematic exogamy enforcement. The Pañji's bilateral 
tracking — essential for sapiṇḍa computation — makes it a bilateral kinship 
database, not merely an agnatic one. 
Third, the Pañji's ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī mechanism — recording normative violations 
rather than suppressing them — is without parallel in other major traditions. 
Where most genealogical systems either ignore transgression or expunge 
deviant records, the Pañji formally systematised the recording of 
transgression as a warning mechanism for future generations, explicitly 
noting that this included inter-caste and inter-religious marriages. 
Fourth, the Pañji's self-referential intellectual genealogy — the documented 
guru-śiṣya transmission chain of Pañjīkāra knowledge — adds a dimension 
of epistemic genealogy that most other traditions lack. The Bhūmikā's 
account of Vidyānanda Jhā's 42-year immersion in the tradition, his father's 
examination by Dr. Sir Gangānātha Jhā, and the transmission protocols at 
Saurath Sabhā constitute a genealogy of genealogical knowledge itself. 
9. Critical Assessment and Theoretical Limitations 
9.1 Patriarchal Bias and Female Invisibility 
Despite the Pañji's comparative openness in recording women, it remains 
fundamentally patrilineal in its primary organisation. Women are recorded as 
daughters (sudhā), wives (through their fathers' identities), and mothers of 
recorded sons — but rarely as individuals with independent genealogical 
standing. The 'sudhāc' notation (also daughters, unnamed) acknowledges 
female existence without full genealogical individuation. From the 
perspective of gender-inclusive genealogical theory (Rubincam 1992; Lechan 
2017), this represents a structural limitation that the Pañji shares with most 
pre-modern genealogical systems. 
The Bhūmikā itself acknowledges this limitation while celebrating partial 
counter-examples: Lakhimā Ṭhakurāin is given full biographical treatment 
precisely because her juridical intervention was historically decisive. The 
Pañji's willingness to record extraordinary women individually, while

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 41 
rendering ordinary women genealogically partial, reflects the same tension 
between structural constraint and exceptional agency that characterises most 
patrilineal genealogical systems. 
9.2 The Hierarchy Problem: Mūla Ranking and Social Power 
The document is candid about a theoretical distortion introduced into the 
Pañji system during the Khaṇḍavalā rule (16th century): the imposition of 
hierarchical ranking among mūlas (high/low mūla categories). The document 
notes that M.M.U. Śaṃkarṣaṇa Ṭhākura, himself of Hariharā mūla, was 
ranked as a groom of Khaṇḍavalā mūla (which later became 'low') — and 
that the Budhavāla mūla (now considered high) had early ancestors who were 
nātiduhitā-putra of the 'low' Suirī mūla. These are manifestly political 
classifications masquerading as genealogical fact. 
From a genealogical theory standpoint, this is what Francoise Zonabend calls 
'genealogical rewriting' — the retrospective manipulation of recorded 
genealogy to serve contemporary social hierarchies. The Pañji's ḍūṣaṇa 
mechanism partly counteracts this by preserving documentary evidence 
against such rewriting, but the hierarchical classifications themselves became 
embedded in the system during the Khaṇḍavalā period. 
9.3 The Oral-Documentary Tension 
The Bhūmikā reveals a persistent tension between oral and documentary 
genealogy within the Pañji tradition. Vidyānanda Jhā's father memorised the 
Utteḍha (the foundational genealogies of major lineages like Mahārāja 
Darbhanga, Suragaṇa Louāma, and Vidyāpati's family) and could recite them 
from memory. This oral layer, now being lost, is genealogically significant 
because it preserves performance contexts, interpretive traditions, and 
relational memories that written texts cannot fully capture. 
The document's lament — 'aba bahuta gote pañjīmeṃ okar carcā saṃ 
bhitare-bhitare gumarata rahait chi' (now many people secretly simmer with 
resentment about the Pañji from within) — signals a crisis of the oral 
transmission layer even as the documentary layer is being digitised. This gap 
between oral genealogical memory and digital genealogical database is a 
challenge that modern genealogical theory has not yet fully theorised for 
South Asian contexts.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 42 
10. The Pañji and Navya-Nyāya Epistemology 
10.1 Genealogical Knowledge as Pramāṇa 
The Bhūmikā does not explicitly invoke Navya-Nyāya (the logical-
epistemological tradition of Maithil scholars, particularly Gaṅgeśa 
Upādhyāya's Tattvacintāmaṇi), but the Pañji system's epistemological 
foundations are deeply consonant with Navya-Nyāya concepts of pramāṇa 
(valid means of knowledge). Navya-Nyāya identifies four pramāṇas: 
pratyakṣa (perception), anumāna (inference), upamāna (comparison), and 
śabda (verbal testimony/reliable authority). 
The Pañji's genealogical claims are primarily śabda-pramāṇa (testimonial 
knowledge) — but structured through a validation system that adds 
inferential (anumāna) cross-checking: if X's mūla is Y, and Y's bījī-puruṣa is 
Z, and the generational depth from Z is known, then X's approximate birth-
century is inferrable. This inferential genealogy is precisely the method by 
which the Pañji's internal chronology was validated against Āryabhaṭa's 
known birth-year. 
10.2 The Pañjīkāra as Prāmāṇika Authority 
In Navya-Nyāya terms, the Pañjīkāra functions as a prāmāṇika — an 
authoritative epistemological agent whose testimony (śabda) generates valid 
knowledge. The institutional conditions for this authority are carefully 
specified: the Pañjīkāra must be dharmaśāstrajña (knowledgeable in 
dharmaśāstra), must have received the guruparaṃparā transmission, and must 
have been formally commissioned by royal authority (rājya-ādeśa). This 
triple qualification — scriptural learning, lineage transmission, and political 
authorisation — constitutes an epistemological warrant structure for 
genealogical testimony that is more rigorous than most modern genealogical 
systems require of their practitioners. 
The document's account of Vidyānanda Jhā's father refusing to grant the 
Pañjī qualification to those who learned it informally ('pāñjī se cīja nahaṃ 
thīka je jethā-tethā sikhala loka siddhānta likhati') reflects this 
epistemological rigour: genealogical knowledge, in the Pañji tradition, is not 
merely technical competence but authoritative transmission within a 
validated lineage of knowers.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 43 
11. Conclusions: The Pañji-Prabandha as a Theory-
Generating System 
This study has demonstrated that the Pañji-Prabandha of Mithila is not 
merely an object of genealogical study but a theory-generating genealogical 
system in its own right. Its contributions to genealogical theory, as 
recoverable from the Bhūmikā to Part 2 of Genome Mapping, include: 
Principal Theoretical Contributions of the Pañji-Prabandha to Genealogical 
Science 
1. Population Genetics Management: The sapiṇḍa/gotra dual-exogamy system 
constitutes a pre-modern institutionalised protocol for managing inbreeding 
coefficients across a bounded population over 1,500+ years — the longest 
continuously documented example in world history. 2. Bilateral Genealogical 
Database: The Pañji's requirement to track both patrilineal (gotra/pravara) and 
bilateral (sapiṇḍa) kinship makes it a bilateral genealogical database — rarer 
and more information-rich than purely agnatic systems. 3. Negative 
Genealogical Attribute System: The ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī's systematic recording of 
normative violations is a unique contribution to genealogical methodology — 
treating transgression as genealogically significant information rather than 
something to be suppressed. 4. Real-Time Legal Genealogy: The Pañji's 
function as a real-time marriage clearance mechanism (not merely 
retrospective record) establishes genealogy as constitutive of social 
reproduction, not merely descriptive of it. 5. Internal Chronological 
Calibration: The generation-interval method (40 years/generation, validated 
against Āryabhaṭa's birth-year) is a sophisticated self-validating chronological 
system antedating modern genealogical proof standards. 6. Migration 
Genealogy at Scale: The Pañji's mūlagrāma records constitute the largest pre-
modern dataset for tracing population migration across multiple centuries in 
South Asia. 7. Feminist Genealogical Consciousness: The systematic (if 
partial) recording of women's names and the juridical role of women scholars 
(Lakhimā Ṭhakurāin) within the institution anticipate modern gender-
inclusive genealogical principles. 8. Epistemic Genealogy: The guru-śiṣya 
transmission chain of Pañjīkāra knowledge constitutes an 'intellectual 
genealogy' that validates the documentary genealogy — a meta-genealogical 
validation structure without clear parallel. 
The authors of Genome Mapping Part 2 — Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra 
Kumar Jhā, and Pañjīkāra Vidyānanda Jhā 'Mohanji' — have produced in this 
Introduction a document that is simultaneously: a historical source, a 
methodological treatise, a comparative genealogy, an ethno-historical survey,

Gajendra Thakur — 44 
a legal analysis, a genetic argument, and a lament for institutional memory at 
risk. The Videha Parallel Literature Movement's systematic preservation and 
digitisation of the Pañji records constitutes, from the perspective of 
genealogical science, one of the most significant cultural heritage projects in 
contemporary South Asia. 
The Pañji-Prabandha awaits full scholarly engagement from genealogical 
theorists, population geneticists, historical demographers, and database 
architects. The present study is offered as a preliminary framework for that 
engagement — demonstrating that when examined through the lens of 
contemporary genealogical theory, the Pañji emerges not as a curiosity of 
caste sociology but as a world-class genealogical information system whose 
theoretical sophistication continues to instruct.

Chapter 3 
The Panji Prabandh of Mithila: Genomic Mapping, 
Theoretical Frameworks, and Socio-Historical 
Trajectories 
The Panji Prabandh of Mithila: Genomic Mapping, Theoretical 
Frameworks, and Socio-Historical Trajectories (450 A.D. – 2009 A.D.) 
Abstract 
The Panji Prabandh (Genealogical Records System) of Mithila stands as one 
of the most comprehensive and oldest unbroken consanguinity registers in 
human history, spanning more than one and a half millennia. Originally 
codified under the Karnata dynasty in 1326 A.D. to systematize family 
lineages among Maithil Brahmins, Kayastha (Karna Kayastha), and 
Kshatriyas, the system served as a rigorous, documentation-based check 
against incestuous marriages and genetic degradation. 
This paper provides a thorough academic analysis of the data contained 
within the seminal volumes compiled by Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra Kumar 
Jha, 
and 
Panjikar 
Vidyanand 
Jha 
"Mohan 
Ji," 
utilizing 
the 
documents"PANJI THEORY MERGED.pdf"and"PRACHIN PANJI 
UPTIP MERGED.pdf". It explores the mathematical-genetic framework of 
the Sapinda exogamy rules, the structural deployment of Gotras and 
Pravaras, the practical apparatus of the Panjikars (genealogists), the 
architectural organization of Mools and Moolgrams, and the sociological 
evolution of the system. 
Furthermore, this study extracts and analyzes extensive historical case 
studies embedded within the Panji records—including the lineages of the 
foundational astronomer Aryabhata, the Navya-Nyaya logician Gangesha 
Upadhyaya, and the iconic poet Vidyapati—alongside a critical examination 
of the Dooshan Panji (records of lineage degradation and inter-caste unions). 
1. Introduction and Historical Origins

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 47 
Long before modern genetic mapping, the cultural and geographical isolation 
of the Mithila region (bounded by the Himalayas and the Ganges River) 
fostered an extraordinarily structured intellectual environment. To protect the 
genetic and cultural matrix of this community, ancient Maithil scholars relied 
on memory and fragmented family registers known as Samuha-Lekhya to 
trace ancestry. The earliest philosophical justification for these registries is 
found in Kumarila Bhatta’s 7th-century Mimamsa treatise, the Tantra-
Varttika, which explicitly states: 
"The noble and cultured preserve their lineage with great effort. For this 
reason, Kings and Brahmins established Samuha-Lekhyani (collective 
written registers) so that the continuity of ancestry from fathers and 
grandfathers is never forgotten, allowing unions to be formed strictly by 
examining the merits and faults of each family." 
By the early 14th century, the informal Samuha-Lekhya system had begun to 
decay. A turning point occurred when Pandit Harinath Upadhyaya, a 
renowned scholar of Dharmashastra and author of the Smritisara, 
inadvertently married his maternal first cousin (his maternal uncle’s 
daughter's daughter) due to a lack of accurate genealogical tracking. 
Recognizing that even the most erudite scholars could no longer track 
complex bloodlines mentally, the contemporary Karnata Ruler of Mithila, 
Maharaja Harisinghadeva, intervened in Shaka Year 1248 (1326 A.D.). He 
decreed that genealogical record-keeping would no longer be a private family 
responsibility but a formalized, state-supervised department run by 
specialized scholars known as Panjikars. 
The system was organized under institutional heads: Pandit Gunakar Jha (a 
scholar from the Mahindra-Pandua Mool) was entrusted with the Brahmin 
registers; Shankardatta was appointed for the Karna Kayasthas; and 
Vijayadatta was placed in charge of the Kshatriyas (specifically the 
Gandhavariya Rajputs). 
2. Theoretical Framework: The Science of Consanguinity&Genome 
Mapping 
The Panji Prabandh acts as an ancient algorithm for genetic exogamy, 
operating through a deeply mathematical system designed to calculate blood 
proximity over multiple generations. Its structural mechanics parallel modern 
genetic pedigree mapping through several layers of biological and social 
classifications.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 48 
2. The Mathematics of Sapinda Proximity and theUtehdFormula 
The core function of the Panji system is to avoid Sapinda (shared-body or 
consanguineous) marriages. According to classical Dharmashastra texts like 
the Manusmriti and Yajnavalkya Smriti, a bride must be Asapinda (not 
sharing a common ancestral body) with the groom. The mathematical 
baseline requires the elimination of the maternal line up to the 5th generation 
and the paternal line up to the 7th generation. 
Under Maharaja Mahesh Thakur, the paternal restriction was modified for 
practical marriageability to an absolute separation of 6 generations, making 
the 7th generation permissible. 
To execute this, the Panjikar reconstructs anUtehd—a comprehensive 
ancestral matrix charting32 specific ancestral linesfor both the prospective 
bride and groom. This matrix maps out the binary lineage tree backward, 
tracking: 
• 
The paternal grandfather and grandmother 
• 
The maternal grandfather and grandmother 
• 
Great-grandparents and great-great-grandparents across all maternal and 
paternal permutations up to 32 foundational ancestors. 
[Prospective Bride / Groom] 
/ \ 
[Paternal Line] [Maternal Line] 
/ \ / \ 
(Father) (Mother) (Maternal (Maternal 
| | Father) Mother) 
[...] [...] | | 
[...] [...] 
(Tracing back to 32 distinct ancestral lineages) 
The system evaluates16 specific generational check-points, known 
colloquially as theChhati(sixth-degree ancestors). For any given individual, 
the 16 Chhatis represent the ultimate structural boundaries of their Sapinda 
zone: 
Chhati No. | Ancestral Identity and Lineage Position PDF 
1 | Paternal Great-Great-Grandfather (Prapitamaha's Paternal Grandfather) 
2 | Father of the Paternal Great-Grandfather’s Paternal Grandmother

The Exclusion Rule:If even a single individual among these 16 Chhatis 
appears within the paternal line of the groom up to the 6th generation, the 
marriage is blocked as incestuous and invalid (Anadhikara). However, if the 
common ancestor appears within the groom's maternal line outside the 5-
generation restriction, the union may be cleared. 
2. Gotras andPravaras 
Superimposed on the Sapinda restrictions is the absolute block on Sagotra 
(same clan) and Sapravara (same invoked sages) marriages. The Maithil 
Brahmin register tracks20 primary Gotras, each linked to a specific 
constellation of Vedic Pravaras (either Tri-Pravara or Pancha-Pravara, 
indicating three or five legendary rishi ancestors who composed Vedic 
hymns): 
• 
Shandilya: Tri-Pravara (Shandilya, Asit, Deval) 
• 
Vatsa: Pancha-Pravara (Orva, Chyavana, Bhargava, Jamadagnya, 
Apnuvana) 
• 
Savarna: Pancha-Pravara (Orva, Chyavana, Bhargava, Jamadagnya, 
Apnuvana) 
• 
Kashyap: Tri-Pravara (Kashyap, Avatsar, Naidhruva) 
• 
Parashar: Tri-Pravara (Shakti, Vashistha, Parashar) 
• 
Bharadwaj: Tri-Pravara (Bharadwaj, Angiras, Barhaspatya) 
• 
Katyayan: Tri-Pravara (Katyayan, Vishnu, Angiras)

they share the exact same Pravara ancestors. Consequently, marriages 
between Vatsa and Savarna individuals are prohibited under the Panji system 
due to their identical ancestral genetic source. 
3. Topographical and Clan Architecture:MoolsandMoolgrams 
To map a massive, growing population over centuries, the Panji system uses 
a geolocation framework based on two coordinates: theMool(the original 
ancestral house or primary clan line) and theMoolgram(the specific village 
or settlement where that branch relocated). 
The system tracks167 uniqueMools, which are broadly divided into three 
tiers based on antiquity and scholarly lineage:AyantShrestha(highest 
tier),Prajayant(second tier), andMadhyam(medium tier).

3. Mapping ofShrotriyaMoolgrams

Decoding the Panji of Mithila

For the elite Shrotriya class, the Panji preserves specific village records: 
• 
Shandilya Gotra: Sarisab (Mool) maps to the Khanguad, Sakuri, and 
Jonki Moolgrams; Parvapalli maps to Vadhiyam; Khandavala maps to 
Bhaur; and Sodarpur maps to Sarisab, Mani, Digaundh, Tetarihar, 
Kanholi Katka, and Hati. 
• 
Vatsa Gotra: Pali maps to Mahishi, Mangrauni, and Jamadauli; Hariamb 
maps to Valirajpur; Ghusaut maps to Nagwad; and Karmaha maps to 
Ruchauli, Naruvad, Behat, Tarauni, Jhanjharpur, and Analpur. 
• 
Kashyap Gotra: Khaual maps to Simvarwad, Murajpur, and Mahua; 
Sakaradhi maps to Parhat and Lohana; Darihara maps to Ratauli; and 
Maandar maps to Sihauli, Rajaura, Kanasama, and Patanuka. 
4. Practical Apparatus: ThePanjikars,Utehd, andSiddhantpatra 
The operational validity of the system rests entirely on state-
appointedPanjikars. 
These 
genealogists 
preserve 
massive, 
bound 
manuscripts written on palm leaves (Talapatra) or specialized paper 
(Basahapatra) in the traditionalTirhuta(Mithilakshar) script. 
Step 1: Family applies to Panjikar 5-10 years before marriage. 
↓ 
Step 2: Panjikar drafts the Utehd (Ancestry Matrix of 32 lines). 
↓ 
Step 3: Cross-verifies the 16 Chhatis for Sapinda/Sagotra conflicts. 
↓ 
Step 4: Public verification at Saurath Sabha Gachhi. 
↓ 
Step 5: Issuance of the Siddhantpatra (Certificate of Exogamy). 
4.1 Process of Verification 
• 
The Request: Several years before an individual reaches marriageable 
age, the family approaches a Panjikar to draw up an Utehd Card 
containing prospective, validated matches. 
• 
Manuscript Cross-Referencing: The Panjikar pulls down their records—
including the historical Mool Panji (the baseline ancestor lists), the 
Shakha Panji (extended branch histories updated down to every newborn

The Assembly Verification: Every summer, during the auspicious

Asvajan Patra or non-kinship certificate). A marriage conducted without 
a verified Siddhantpatra is declared uncanonical and illegitimate 
(Ashastriya). 
5. Extensive Case Studies of Historical Lineages 
The Panji manuscripts provide detailed records of historical figures, serving 
as an important tool for verifying chronology in Indian history. Below are 
transcriptions and structural analyses of the family trees of key scientists, 
logicians, and poets found in the registers.

• 
Islamic/Yavana Integration: The registers document marriages involving 
elite Muslim women. For instance, the Pabauli Mool record notes that 
Dagar, the son of Pandit Keshava, married Dhaulati Jahan, a Yavana 
woman. Their son, Harihar, was recorded directly into the registry 
without facing social exclusion. Similarly, the Kujouli Mool records the 
line of Gopal, whose son Lakhusha also married into the family of

• 
The Legend of Gangesha's Birth: The entry for Gangesha Upadhyaya in 
the Dooshan Panji contains a notable note: "Pitri Parokshe Pancha 
Varsha Vyatite Gangeshotpatti". This explicitly states that Gangesha was 
born five years after his father had passed away, noting that his mother, 
Vallabha, was a Charmakarini (from the shoemaker caste). The Panji 
records this union openly, alongside Gangesha's output of the 
Tattvacintamani, without removing him from the scholarly register. 
• 
The Chamar/Leatherworker Unions: The Talhanpur Mool records that 
Ananda a leatherworker woman (Charmakarini) got married , and their 
son, Diwakar, was preserved in the records. Similarly, the Mandar Mool 
records Hirua, a Chamar individual whose daughter married into the elite 
Alei Mool. 
• 
The Rajak/Washerman Unions: The Khaual Mool records Rajeshwar, 
who married a washerman's daughter (Rajak Kanya). Additionally, the 
Sodarpur Mool traces the lines of Dharam and Maniki, noting a long-
standing Rajak relationship within their ancestry. 
• 
The Koch Royalty Union: The Khandavala Mool register records that the 
daughter of Pandit Chandraputi married into the Koch Rajvanshi royal 
family of Assam, bringing Princess Sohago into the lineage. 
7. Sociological Evolution: Stratification and Social Ramifications

While the Panji Prabandh was designed as a tool for genetic exogamy, its 
implementation over the centuries brought deep social changes and 
unintended consequences to Mithila. 
7. internal Stratification:Shrotriya, Jog, andJaibar 
The original deployment under Maharaja Harisinghadeva treated all families 
as structurally equal within their respective caste registers. However, during 
the reign of Maharaja Madhav Singh (mid-to-late 18th century), the 
introduction of theShakhaPanji completely transformed Maithil society. 
A complex, hierarchical classification emerged, breaking the community into 
three strict tiers: 
• 
Shrotriya (Soti): The highest social tier, comprising families with 
impeccable records of scriptural scholarship and zero lineage blemishes 
across generations. Their registers are referred to as Laukit registers, split 
across 8 primary Chaugoala tiers and 32 dynamic sub-panjis. 
• 
Jog (Yogya): The intermediate category, consisting of families with 
minor documented Dooshan infractions or lineages that had slightly 
diverged from the highest standard of scholarship. 
• 
Jaibar (Vanshaja): The baseline populace tier. Though their lineages 
were fully documented and verified for marriage validity, they did not 
receive the elite social privileges granted to the Shrotriyas. They track 15 
distinct social tiers using 153 specific Panji indices (such as the Narapati 
Jha Panji). 
7.2 Social Ramifications: Hypergamy, Child Marriage, and 
Widowhood 
This rigid internal hierarchy distorted the marriage market through 
hypergamy (Anuloma unions). Because marrying a daughter into a higher-
tier Shrotriya family raised the social standing of her entire clan, demand for 
Shrotriya grooms spiked dramatically. This dynamic triggered severe social 
issues across Mithila: 
• 
The Rise of Polygamy: Wealthy, high-tier Shrotriya men began marrying 
dozens of women from lower Jaibar or Jog tiers, often treating marriage 
as a financial arrangement driven by substantial dowries. 
• 
Child Marriages: Families raced to marry off daughters at very young 
ages to secure alliances with high-status grooms before the marriage 
market shifted.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 60 
• 
The Crisis of Widowhood: Because old, higher-tier men frequently 
married young girls, a large demographic wave of child widows emerged 
across Mithila. Due to strict religious prohibitions against widow 
remarriage at the time, these girls faced a lifetime of social isolation. 
8. Conclusion and Modern Transition to Digital Conservation 
The Panji Prabandh represents an early, highly structured approach to genetic 
genealogy, tracking bloodlines with precision long before the advent of 
modern computers or DNA sequencing. It effectively prevented inbreeding 
depression within a concentrated population across generations. 
However, when the system shifted from its original focus on genetic health 
toward enforcing social status and hierarchy, it introduced deep social 
imbalances, including hypergamy and child marriage, which affected Maithil 
society for generations. 
Today, the Panji system faces an existential threat. The decline of traditional 
scripts like *Tirhuta(Mithilakshar)* has left younger generations unable to 
read the original palm-leaf records. 
To protect this historical resource, digital conservation efforts began in 2000 
A.D.. Over 10, historic palm-leaf, Basahapatra, and paper manuscripts—
spanning over 1, years of genealogical data—have been digitally imaged, 
mapped onto modern storage media, and transliterated into the widely 
accessible Devanagari script. This ensures that one of humanity's deepest 
genealogical archives remains preserved and open to historical and genetic 
research.

Architectural and Geo-Cultural Framework of Mithila's Varna 
State 
The region of Mithila, historically recognized under the classical 
nomenclatures of Videha, Tirabhukti, Tirhut, or Mithilanchal, is a 
geographically and culturally distinct territory bounded by the Himalayas in 
the north, the Ganges River in the south, the Gandak River in the west, and 
the Mahananda River in the east. From antiquity, this region has served as an 
intellectual crucible for the development of Vedic, Upanishadic, and classical 
Sanskrit learning, nurturing major orthodox philosophical systems, including 
Nyaya, Vaisheshika, Mimamsa, and Samkhya. 
Following the dissolution of the Pala dynasty in Bengal, Mithila transitioned 
into a socio-political order dominated by high-ranking landowning and 
priestly lineages. In this unique administrative landscape, the Maithil 
Brahmins assumed positions as both Bhudevas (landowners) and Bhupalas 
(monarchs or state protectors), effectively unifying temporal and spiritual 
authority. To preserve this concentrated authority and defend the orthodox 
social order against external socio-religious changes, the regional dynasties 
established the Panji Prabandh—an exceptionally comprehensive system of 
written, state-regulated genealogical record-keeping.

Prior to the state-sponsored codification of the Panji Prabandh in the 
fourteenth century, the preservation of lineage was decentralized. Families 
recorded their ancestry through oral transmission or localized, family-held 
manuscripts known as Samuh Lekhya. The seventh-century Mimamsa 
scholar Kumarila Bhatta, in his monumental philosophical work Tantra 
Varttika, explicitly notes that elite families maintained Samuh Lekhya as a 
formal mechanism to track virtues, defects, and ancestral lineages across 
generations, ensuring the preservation of caste identity. 
However, by the late thirteenth and early fourteenth centuries, these 
decentralized records had suffered significant decay, leading to genealogical 
amnesia within families. This loss of historical memory made it difficult to 
verify the exogamic restrictions mandated by the Smriti texts, resulting in 
accidental violations of marriage laws. This growing social crisis eventually 
prompted the royal administration to intervene. 
Historical Catalysts and the Legend of Harinath Upadhyaya 
The formal codification of the Panji Prabandh took place during the reign of 
Maharaja Harisimhadeva (r. 1307–1324 AD), the last sovereign of the Karnat 
dynasty of Mithila. While the political stability of the Karnat state was 
disrupted in 1324–1325 AD by the military campaign of Delhi Sultan 
Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq—forcing Maharaja Harisimhadeva to withdraw to the 
Nepal hills—the structural framework of the genealogical registry had 
already been established under his patronage. 
The immediate catalyst for this administrative initiative was a highly 
publicized social scandal involving Pandit Harinath Upadhyaya, an eminent 
scholar of Dharmashastra and the author of the Smritisarasangraha. Due to

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 64 
the degradation of family records, Harinath Upadhyaya unknowingly married 
a woman who fell within the prohibited degrees of consanguinity—
specifically, his maternal cousin’s daughter (saakshat pitayaut bhaik 
dauhitri). Sometime after the wedding, Upadhyaya's wife was accused of 
infidelity with an individual of a lower caste. 
To assert her innocence and prove her marital fidelity, she was subjected to a 
traditional hot-iron ordeal. Despite her absolute physical innocence, her 
hands were severely burned during the test. 
The failure of a chaste woman to pass a divine ordeal prompted an intense 
inquiry by the court pandits under Maharaja Harisimhadeva. The 
investigation revealed that while she had been physically faithful to her 
husband, her hands had burned because she had committed the sin of incest 
by marrying within prohibited kinship degrees (swajana). Realizing that the 
absence of written genealogies left the community vulnerable to accidental 
incestuous marriages, Maharaja Harisimhadeva ordered the creation of a 
systematic genealogical register for every family in the Maithil Brahmin and 
Karna Kayastha communities. 
This state-sponsored census was initiated around Shaka 1248 (corresponding 
to 1326–1327 AD). The King convened the historic Vishwachakra 
Sammelan at Jamsam, located in the Pandaul region of Madhubani, where 
genealogists gathered family trees from every village. 
To manage this archive, the King established a state department of genealogy 
and appointed specialized registrars known as Panjikars. Sadupadhyaya 
Gunakar Jha, of the Madhudhepur Pandua lineage, was appointed as the first 
Panjikar for the Brahmins. Simultaneously, Shankardutt was appointed to 
manage the records of the Karna Kayasthas, and Vijayadutt was appointed 
for the Kshatriyas, specifically the Gandhavariya Rajputs. 
The female scholar Thakurain Lakhima, a contemporary of Harisimhadeva 
celebrated for her expertise in Dharmashastra, also played a key role in 
developing this institutional framework, contributing her scholarly authority 
to the design of the registry. 
Taxonomic Architecture: Gotras, Mools, and Shakhas 
The Panji Prabandh identifies and maps family lines using three primary 
coordinates: the Biji Purush (progenitor seed-man), the Mool (original 
ancestral village of the Biji Purush), and the Moolgram/Shakha (subsequent

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 65 
village migrations). The Biji Purush represents the earliest historically 
verifiable ancestor of a specific patrilineage. The Moolgram identifies the 
geographical village where this progenitor resided at the time of the 
fourteenth-century census. As descendants migrated over time, these new 
settlements were recorded as Shakhas (branches) and appended to the 
original Mool. 
For example, the Kaushik-gotra Mandar mool split into branches like the 
Mangroni and Garh shakhas. Similarly, the Khandila mool, associated with 
the Khadauray lineage, originally originated from the Gangoli (Gangulivar) 
mool. When the medieval scholar Mahamahopadhyaya Shankarshan Thakur 
established his academy in the Khanda province (modern Madhya Pradesh), 
his descendants lived there for several generations. In the eighth generation, 
Surapati Thakur returned to Mithila, and his descendants were categorized 
under the Khandila mool’s Ghoshi branch. 
At its inception, the Panji registry classified the Maithil Brahmin community 
into 20 Gotras (clans) representing patrilineal descent from ancient Vedic 
sages, containing 167 primary Moolgrams. Within these, 34 original mools—
which later expanded to 35 with the inclusion of the Khanam branch of the 
Phanandah mool—were recognized as prestigious high-ranking lineages.

Beyond patrilineal gotra associations, the Panji Prabandh divided the Maithil 
Brahmin community into ranked hierarchies based on their ritual adherence 
and academic lineage 6: 
• 
Shrotriya: The highest ranked class, composed of families that 
maintained absolute adherence to Vedic sacraments and scripture, 
historically requiring the abandonment of five maternal and seven 
paternal generations in marriage unions. 
• 
Yogya: The respectable class, possessing high intellectual and ritual 
status, but ranked slightly below the Shrotriyas. 
• 
Panjibaddh (or Vanshaj): Registered lineages whose marriages strictly 
conformed to the standardized maternal five-generation and paternal six-
generation exogamy rules.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 67 
• 
Jaibar (or Jayabar): Families whose records were either incomplete 
during the initial registration or who suffered lower social status due to 
non-standard or un-Shastric marriage alliances. 
This internal hierarchical system, while initially intended to promote ritual 
discipline, was made highly rigid during the eighteenth century under 
Maharaja Madhav Singh of the Khandavala dynasty (Darbhanga Raj). 
Madhav Singh introduced the Shakha Panji (branch registries) and 
formalized ranked classifications. This institutionalized hypergamy (similar 
to the Kulin system of Bengal), creating social divisions and sub-castes 
within the community. 
The Mathematical Mechanics of Matrimony and the Sixteen Chhathis 
The primary function of the Panji Prabandh is to govern marriages by verifying that 
no consanguineous relationship exists between the prospective bride and groom. To 
achieve this, the Panjikar constructs a comprehensive genealogical matrix known as 
the Utedh. The Utedh maps a 32-ancestor bilateral tree spanning up to six 
generations on the paternal side and five generations on the maternal side. 
The mathematical progression governing this ancestral tracking across

is modeled as: 
 
For the maternal side, where

ancestors to verify lineage. This structural configuration ensures that the fundamental 
exogamic axiom of Hindu law—Matritah Panchami Tyaktwa, Pitritah Saptami 
Bhajet (avoiding five generations on the mother's side and seven on the father's 
side)—is mathematically satisfied.9 In the modified practice established by 
Mahamahopadhyaya Mahesh Thakur during the sixteenth century, the paternal 
restriction was adjusted to six generations, while the maternal remained strictly at 
five. 
To verify non-consanguinity, the Panjikar must trace the bride’s descent from 
sixteen distinct, highly specific ancestral nodes known as the Sixteen Chhathis. If 
any of these sixteen Chhathi ancestors of the bride appear within the prohibited 
generations of the groom's paternal or maternal pedigree, the prospective union is 
classified as Swajana (consanguineous/incestuous) and is legally and ritually barred.

Operational Walkthrough of an Utedh Calculation 
To illustrate the application of these rules, the following case study analyzes 
a proposed marriage between a bride from the Karamaha mool and a groom 
from the Darihara mool. 
The bride is the daughter of Shashinath Jha and the granddaughter of 
Pitambar Jha, belonging to the Karamaha mool, Behat moolgram, and 
residing in the village of Vittho (Darbhanga). Her maternal grandfather is 
Narayandutt Thakur of the Khandabala mool, Bhaur moolgram, residing in 
Dharampur. 
The groom is the son of Krishnanand Jha and the grandson of Gopinand Jha, 
belonging to the Darihara mool, Ratauli moolgram, and residing in Lohana 
village. The groom is the maternal grandson of Kanhaiya Jha, of the 
Karamaha mool, Behat moolgram, residing in Vittho. 
The Panjikar evaluates the sixteen Chhathi nodes of the bride to ensure no 
overlap occurs with the groom's lineage 9: 
• 
First Chhathi: The paternal great-grandfather’s father is Vishwanath Jha, 
son of Shankardutt, of the Karamaha mool. Tracing the descent shows 
that his son is Radhanath, grandson is Kantir (Revatinath), great-
grandson is Pitambar, and great-great-granddaughter is the bride. 
Vishwanath Jha is thus identified as the First Chhathi. 
• 
Second Chhathi: Dhanapati Jha, son of Indrapati Jha of the Madari 
Valias mool, who is the father-in-law of Radhanath Jha, is identified as 
the Second Chhathi. 
• 
Third Chhathi: Raghuvar Jha, of the Mandar Sihauli mool, is identified 
as the Third Chhathi. He is the maternal grandfather’s father of Pitambar 
Jha, with the lineage progressing through his son Feeku Jha, daughter's 
husband Kantir Jha, grandson Pitambar, and great-granddaughter (the 
bride). 
• 
Fourth Chhathi: Harshi Jha, of the Pali Mahishi mool, is identified as the 
Fourth Chhathi. He is the father-in-law of Feeku Jha.

carefully calculate the exact generational distance from their common

Typology of Panji Manuscripts and Preservation Traditions 
The Panji Prabandh archive is divided into seven distinct types of specialized 
registers, each serving a specific function within the genealogical system 9: 
• 
Mool Panji: The foundational, comprehensive pedigree register that 
records the main family trees of the entire community, organized by their 
Biji Purush and original villages. 
• 
Shakha Panji: The branched registry system introduced during the reign 
of Maharaja Madhav Singh, which tracks the migration of families to 
secondary settlements (shakhas) and records social stratifications. 
• 
Gotra Panji: A specialized index organized by the 20 Gotras, 
documenting the ancient villages and sages associated with each clan. 
• 
Patra Panji: A territorial registry system established approximately 500 
years ago, which records family histories by village names, facilitating 
spatial tracking. 
• 
Dushan Panji: A register that documents social and ritual decline, 
recording past un-Shastric marriages or other transgressions to warn 
families and protect the community's ritual standing. 
• 
Utedh Panji: The register used to verify sapinda-exogamy, mapping 
paternal and maternal lineages to ensure marriages conform to Smriti 
regulations. 
• 
Aswajanyapatra Panji: A specialized registry containing copies of the 
issued certificates of non-consanguinity, serving as a legal and social 
record of approved marriages. 
An exceptional exception within this system is the Rasadh-Araria Panji, 
which is the only registry that systematically records the names of female 
ancestors. In other registers, female names are omitted, and maternal lines 
are tracked through their fathers' names. 
Because these manuscripts were written on fragile palm leaves and 
handmade paper, the Panjikars developed strict preservation protocols. A 
traditional Sanskrit conservation mantra was used to guide the care of the 
documents: 
This translates to: "Protect the manuscript from water, protect it from oil, and 
protect it from loose or poor binding." 9 Additionally, the Panjikars were

constellation of Leo (Singh Rashi, between August 16 and September 15). 
The atmospheric heat during this late-summer period is exceptionally dry and 
powerful, making it ideal for drying the fragile leaves without making them 
brittle. 
The Spatial Ecology of Sabhagachis and the Matrimonial 
Assemblies 
To put this complex genealogical verification system into practice, the 
sovereigns of Mithila established fourteen designated, state-supervised 
venues known as Sabhagachis. These Sabhagachis were large, open-air 
campuses situated in sacred mango groves, where thousands of families, 
scholars, and Panjikars gathered annually during the auspicious wedding 
seasons.

remains active and is celebrated as Saurath Sabha 4

annual marriage assemblies 8

During these assemblies, families sat under the shade of mango trees while 
Panjikars consulted their palm-leaf registers to cross-reference the lineages of 
prospective couples. Once the Panjikars confirmed that the proposed union 
met all exogamic requirements, they issued the Siddhanta Patra in public, 
allowing the wedding to proceed. 
These assemblies served not only as matrimonial markets but also as cultural 
and intellectual centers where scholars engaged in public debates 
(Shastrartha). To ensure the integrity of this system, Panjikars had to pass the 
Dhaut Pariksha, a rigorous ten-year course of study and examination held at 
the court of the Darbhanga Raj. Successful candidates were awarded the title

of Panjikar and honored by the state, establishing a highly respected class of 
professional genealogists. 
Bio-Anthropological Insights and Modern Genomic Parallelisms 
From a bio-anthropological perspective, the Panji Prabandh represents a 
highly sophisticated pre-modern system of genetic mapping and public health 
management. Although medieval lawgivers framed the Panji rules in terms of 
ritual purity and religious duty, the system is deeply aligned with the 
scientific principles of avoiding close-kin mating. 
In small, endogamous communities, marriages between close relatives 
significantly increase the coefficient of inbreeding (), leading to high 
homozygosity of deleterious recessive alleles. This causes inbreeding 
depression, which manifests as congenital abnormalities, developmental 
delays, and weakened immune systems. 
By enforcing strict, multi-generational bilateral exogamy, the Panji Prabandh 
effectively prevented the concentration of these harmful recessive genes, 
protecting the genetic health of the community. 
A major strength of the Panji system is its bilateral approach. While many 
patriarchal societies only track the father's direct patrilineal line, the Utedh 
and the sixteen Chhathis place equal emphasis on maternal lineages. 
By tracking maternal ancestors across five generations, the system monitors 
mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) lines and prevents the concentration of rare 
maternal genetic disorders. 
Additionally, because the system tracks complex female-male ancestral 
crossings up to five generations, it limits autosomal genetic sharing, keeping 
the coefficient of kinship between partners low. 
The massive scale of this archive is demonstrated by the research of modern 
historians and genealogists. The transcription of over 11, palm-leaf 
manuscripts by scholars Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra Kumar Jha, and 
Panjikar Vidyanand Jha shows that the Panji records trace uninterrupted 
lineages of millions of individuals from approximately 450 AD to 2009 AD. 
This represents one of the largest continuous human pedigree databases in 
existence, predating and rivaling modern national genealogical databases. 
The Panji is not merely a list of names; it serves as a demographic record, 
documenting ancestral migrations, historical educational titles (such as

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 74 
Upadhyaya, Mahamahopadhyaya, and Pandit), and physical relocations 
across six centuries. 
Contemporary Crisis and the Future of Mithila's Archives 
Despite its historical, scientific, and cultural significance, the Panji Prabandh 
faces severe challenges in the modern era. Changing lifestyles, urbanization, 
and global migration have scattered the Maithil Brahmin and Karna Kayastha 
populations, distancing younger generations from these traditional customs. 
The practice of gathering at Sabhagachis has declined significantly, and 
many families now rely on digital matchmaking platforms and horoscope 
software, which lack the deep lineage verification of the Utedh. 
This decline has also had a severe impact on the Panjikars themselves. 
Historically, becoming a Panjikar required a demanding ten-year course of 
study at the court of the Darbhanga Raj, culminating in the rigorous Dhaut 
Pariksha examination. Scholars who passed this state examination were 
awarded the prestigious status of a licensed genealogist. 
Following the dissolution of the Darbhanga Raj, the Dhaut Pariksha was 
discontinued, and the profession became largely hereditary, lacking formal 
institutional training. Because the profession offers little financial stability in 
the modern economy, younger members of Panjikar families are increasingly 
pursuing other careers, leaving only a small number of elderly registrars to 
maintain these massive archives. 
The physical preservation of the genealogical records is also a matter of 
urgent concern. The vast majority of these 700-year-old records are written 
in the traditional Mithilakshar (or Tirhuta) and Kaithi scripts on fragile palm 
leaves and aging, handmade paper. These manuscripts are highly vulnerable 
to humidity, dust, insect damage, and daily wear and tear. 
In the absence of a systematic, state-funded archiving program, centuries of 
valuable genetic, demographic, and sociological history are at risk of being 
lost forever. Scholars and cultural organizations have emphasized that there 
is an urgent need to digitize, catalog, and preserve these palm-leaf records. 
Digitizing the Panji would not only preserve Mithila's cultural heritage but 
would also create an invaluable resource for modern genetic research, 
enabling bio-medical studies to map hereditary diseases and population 
genetics across South Asia.

The Socio-Genetic Architecture of Mithila: An Exhaustive 
Anthropological and Historical Analysis of the Panji Prabandha 
(450 AD – 2009 AD) 
Introduction and Regional Context 
The cultural and geographical region of Mithila, historically designated as 
Videha, Tirabhukti, Tirhut, or Mithilanchal, is situated between the foothills 
of the Himalayas to the north and the Ganges River to the south, bounded by 
the Gandaki River to the west and the Mahananda River to the east. For 
millennia, this territory has served as a primary sanctuary for Vedic and 
Upanishadic scholarship, fostering unique socio-legal systems that governed 
local populations under strict shastric frameworks. Politically, Mithila’s 
history is marked by a succession of influential dynasties, notably the Pala 
Empire, the Karnata Dynasty, the Oiniwar Dynasty, and the Khandavala 
Dynasty (ruling as the Darbhanga Raj). 
Within this historical framework, the social hierarchy of Mithila evolved a 
distinct power dynamic characterized by the convergence of Bhudevas 
(landowners) and Bhupalas (land protectors). In Mithila, the priestly class 
and the ruling class closely aligned their authority, with the monarchs serving 
as both secular administrators and protectors of the orthodox Brahmanical 
social order. To preserve this structure against socio-cultural shifts and 
external invasions, the state instituted the Panji Prabandha—an extensive, 
highly structured system of genealogical record-keeping maintained for the 
Maithil Brahmins and Karna Kayasthas. This system established strict 
endogamous boundaries through state-enforced marital regulations, making it

Gajendra Thakur — 76 
one of the most comprehensive genealogical and genetic archiving systems 
in human history. 
Historical Foundations and the Crisis of 1326 AD 
Prior to the formalization of the Panji Prabandha, genealogical tracking in 
Mithila was decentralized and maintained through oral traditions and 
informal family records known as Uteadh. Marital alliances relied on the 
recollection of ancestral lineages up to a few generations and the matching of 
Gotras. However, as the population expanded and migrated across different 
river systems, these oral networks proved insufficient to prevent accidental 
endogamous violations of prohibited degrees of kinship (Sapinda).

The institutionalization of the Panji Prabandha was precipitated by a major 
socio-religious crisis in $1326\text{ AD}$. Pandit Harinath Upadhyaya, a 
highly respected authority on Dharma-shastra and the author of the Smritisar, 
resided in the village of Satadhara. Lacking detailed written records of his 
maternal lineage, he inadvertently married his cousin—specifically, his 
paternal uncle’s daughter’s daughter (pitayaut bhaik dauhitri). 
The transgressive nature of this union was exposed following an incident 
where an "untouchable" man of the Dusadh caste approached Pandit 
Harinath's wife. Though she was vindicated through a trial by ordeal—where 
her chastity was tested by fire—subsequent investigations in the royal court 
revealed that the marriage itself violated sacred law. Under shastric 
jurisprudence, a marriage within prohibited Sapinda degrees was considered

invalid, and any offspring of such a union were classified as Chandala, 
leading to exclusion from the Aryan social fold. 
Recognizing that even the most learned scholars could no longer accurately 
trace their lineages orally, the last ruler of the Karnat Dynasty, King 
Harisimhadeva (reigned 1304–1325/1326 AD), intervened. In Sake 1248 
(corresponding to $1326\text{ AD}$), the king decreed that the state would 
take over the documentation of family histories. He convened a grand 
assembly of scholars, known as the Vishwachakra Sammelan, at Jamsam 
(Pandaul, Madhubani district) in $1327\text{ AD}$. 
At this assembly, King Harisimhadeva appointed official genealogists, 
known as Panjikars, who were tasked with conducting a comprehensive 
census of every family’s lineage, gotra, pravara, ancestral village, and 
migrated settlements. This registry was designed to ensure that no future 
marriage could occur within the prohibited degrees of kinship.

Saduphyadhyaya Gunakar Jha of the Mahendrapur-Pandua lineage was

The Panji system was later patronized by subsequent ruling dynasties, 
including the Oiniwar dynasty and the Khandavala dynasty (Darbhanga Raj). 
To maintain high standards, the Darbhanga Raj established a ten-year 
academic program. Aspiring Panjikars had to pass a rigorous examination, 
the Dhaut Pariksha, after which they were formally authorized as registrars. 
Over time, following the dissolution of the Darbhanga Raj, this system 
transitioned into a hereditary guild.

Socio-Ritual Divisions and Sub-Caste Formations 
The implementation of the Panji Prabandha codified existing ritual and 
philosophical distinctions within the Maithil Brahmin community. At its 
core, the community was divided along Vedic and ritual lines into two 
groups : 
• 
vājasaneyī (Vajasaneyi): Followers of the Madhyandina branch of the 
White Yajurveda. 
• 
chandoga (Chandogya): Followers of the Kauthuma branch of the 
Samaveda. 
These ritual divisions are marked by distinct lifecycle ceremonies (Sanskara), 
particularly during the Upanayana (sacred thread ceremony) and the Vivaha 
(marriage rituals) : 
Lifecycle Ceremony | Vajasaneyi (Yajurvedi) Practice | Chandogya 
(Samavedi) Practice

(tonsure) 2. Upanayana (investiture) 3. Vedarambha (initiation into Vedic 
study) 4. Samavartana (graduation) | Comprises four distinct rituals: 1. 
Namakarana (naming) 2. Chudakarana (tonsure) 3. Upanayana (investiture) 
4. Samavartana (graduation) 
Vivaha (Marriage) Structure | Conducted as a single, continuous ceremony 
that can be performed during either the day or the night. | Divided into two 
distinct parts: 1. Purva-vivah (initial marriage) 2. Uttara-vivah (concluding 
marriage), performed strictly after sunset after viewing the star Arundhati. 
At a broader level, Mithila's codification reflects the wider regional 
classification of Indian Brahmins. According to puranic frameworks, the 
Brahmin community of India is divided into ten categories, separated 
geographically by the Vindhya Range into the Pancha-Gauda (Northern) and 
Pancha-Dravida (Southern) groups :

Within this framework, Maithil Brahmins developed a distinct identity 
shaped by their unique geography, dietary habits, language, and the local

Taxonomic Architecture: Gotras, Mulas, and Mulgrams 
The structural integrity of the Panji Prabandha relies on three key concepts: 
the Biji Purush (the seed ancestor), the Mula (the ancestral village), and the 
Mulgram (the village of migration). 
The Biji Purush is the earliest identifiable ancestor of a lineage, serving as 
the root of the family tree. The Mula represents the original territorial 
settlement where this Biji Purush resided at the time of the $1327\text{ 
AD}$ census. If a family migrated to a new village, their new residence was 
recorded as the Mulgram, which was appended to their original Mula to track 
the family’s geographic movement (e.g., Mandar-Garh or Mandar-
Mangarouni). 
At the time of the census, the community was classified into twenty 
patrilineal Gotras (clans). Each Gotra is associated with specific Pravaras 
(groups of Vedic rishis) and original Mulas.

The Mechanics of Exogamy: Utedh and Sapinda Calculations

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 82 
The primary function of the Panji Prabandha is to enforce strict exogamy 
rules to prevent inbreeding within the relatively small, endogamous Maithil 
community. It strictly prohibits unions between individuals who share a 
common ancestor within a specified number of generations. 
To calculate these prohibited degrees, the Panjikars use a standardized 
genealogical framework known as the Utedh. This tool maps out $32$ 
distinct ancestral lines of both the prospective bride and groom, representing 
the paternal and maternal grandparents of their ancestors. 
│ 
┌───────────────────────┴──────────────────
─────┐ 
▼ ▼ 
[Paternal Lineage] [Maternal Lineage] 
(Exogamous Avoidance) (Exogamous Avoidance) 
│ │ 
▼ ▼ 
Paternal Generation $\ge 6$ Maternal Generation $\ge 5$ 
The mathematical rule governing marriageability is expressed through 
generation avoidance limits : 
$$\text{Sapinda Avoidance Limit} = \begin{cases} \text{Generation } (g_p) 
\ge 6, & \text{on Paternal side} \\ \text{Generation } (g_m) \ge 5, & \text{on 
Maternal side} \end{cases}$$ 
Historically, under classical Dharma-shastra frameworks (such as the Manu 
Smriti), the paternal avoidance limit was seven generations. However, in the 
sixteenth century, Maharaja Mahesh Thakur modified the paternal limit to six 
generations for practical matching purposes within the community, a 
standard that has since been followed. 
To verify eligibility, the Panjikar cross-references the lineages of both parties 
against sixteen ancestral nodes, known as the Chhatis (Sixteenths). These 
nodes represent the sixteen possible maternal and paternal ancestral roots of 
an individual : 
Chhati Node | Genealogical Definition and Kinship Path 
1 | Bride's paternal great-great-grandfather (Vriddha-prapitama-prapitama)

An alliance is deemed invalid (Aswajan) if any ancestor within these sixteen

To illustrate the mathematical application of this system, consider the historic

involved the daughter of Shashinath Jha (of the Karmah-Behat lineage,

the daughter of Narayandatt Thakur (of the Khadabala-Bhaur lineage). The 
groom’s father was Krishnanand Jha, whose paternal grandfather was

This overlap placed the bride and groom within the prohibited sixth 
generation of relationship on the paternal side. Consequently, the union was 
disqualified, and the Panjikar refused to issue the Siddhanta Patra, protecting 
the community's exogamous rules. 
Sakha Panji and Social Stratification 
In $1327\text{ AD}$, the original Panji Prabandha was established on an 
egalitarian basis, recording all families with equal standing. However, in the

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 85 
mid-eighteenth century, during the reign of Maharaja Madhav Singh, the 
system underwent a major structural shift with the introduction of the Sakha 
Panji (branch registries). This new framework divided the Maithil Brahmin 
community into three hierarchically stratified classes based on perceived 
lineage purity and ritual adherence : 
• 
Shrotriya (Soti): The highest class, characterized by strict endogamous 
marriages and rigorous performance of Vedic rituals. 
• 
Yogya (Jog): The middle class, comprising families of respected 
scholarly standing. 
• 
Panjibaddha: The basic registered class, consisting of families of 
standard lineage. 
Families not registered in these three categories were classified asJaibar. 
The introduction of this hierarchy led to significant social issues. It fostered a 
system of hypergamy, where families from lower tiers sought alliances with 
Shrotriya men to elevate their social status. This practice contributed to 
polygamy, child marriage, and a rising number of young widows, as older 
Shrotriya men frequently married multiple younger women from lower-status 
families. 
Under this system, the elite Shrotriya class was organized into eight ritual 
tiers, known as Chaugolas (quadrants), which regulated marriages across 
thirty-two specific registries :

The Dushana Panji: Social Adaptations and Genetic Anomalies 
To document and manage lineage deviations, the Panjikars compiled a 
separate volume known as the Dushana Panji (the Register of Blemishes). 
This register served as an unfiltered record of social and biological history, 
documenting cases of inter-caste marriages, non-traditional unions, and 
hereditary medical disorders.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 87 
Among its most significant entries are records concerning prominent 
historical figures, some of which were omitted from later traditionalist 
publications to preserve orthodox narratives.

The register contains a detailed entry for Gangesha Upadhyaya, the founder 
of the Navya-Nyaya school of logic and author of the Tattvachintamani. The 
original Panji manuscripts state that Gangesha was born five years after the 
death of his legal father (pitri parokshe panch varsh vyatite). Additionally, 
the text records that his mother was a Charmakarini (a woman from the 
leather-working caste). While these details were omitted from later

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 88 
genealogies compiled by twentieth-century scholars, the original palm-leaf 
manuscripts preserve these records of his ancestry. 
The Dushana Panji also provides a clear lineage for the astronomer-
mathematician Aryabhata, tracing his family back to the Mandar Mula 
(Mangeroni branch). This lineage begins with the Biji ancestor 
Trinayanbhatta (c. $400\text{ AD}$) and details fifteen generations of 
descendants : 
$$\text{Trinayanbhatta (Biji)} \rightarrow \text{Udayabhata} \rightarrow 
\text{Vijayabhata} \rightarrow \text{Sulochana} \rightarrow \text{Bhatta} 
\rightarrow \text{Dharmajati Mishra} \rightarrow \text{Dharajati Mishra}$$ 
$$\rightarrow \text{Brahmaraj Mishra} \rightarrow \text{Tripurajati Mishra} 
\rightarrow \text{Vidhujati Mishra} \rightarrow \text{Ajaysingh} \rightarrow 
\text{Vijaysingh} \rightarrow \text{Adivaraha}$$ 
$$\rightarrow \text{Mahovaraha} \rightarrow \text{Duryodhan Singh} 
\rightarrow \text{Mahamohopadhyaya Narasimha (Weapon Specialist)}$$ 
This record places Aryabhata's family within the historical intellectual 
landscape of ancient Mithila. 
Similarly, the Panji traces the spiritual lineage of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu to 
Ramapati Upadhyaya (also known as Vishnupuri or Paramanandapuri) of the 
Vatsa gotra and Karmah-Tarauni lineage, documenting the connections 
between medieval Maithil scholars and the wider Bhakti movement in 
Bengal. 
The Dushana Panji also contains records of numerous other non-traditional 
unions, which were documented to track their long-term genetic and social 
impacts on the community :

Integration and Generational Resolution

origin. | Descendants integrated into the main lineage after six generations of

unknown caste. | Descendants classified under the Dushana registry but 
retained in the community.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 89

| Descendants reintegrated into the main lineage after several generations. 
Khadabala (Surapati Branch) | Union with Sohago, daughter of the Koch

Sodarpur (Govind Branch) | Union with Medha, a woman of the Charmakar

The register also contains records of marriages with women from the 
Devadasi (temple dancer) tradition, such as the mothers of Jagannath 
(Satalakha Mula), Ratanu (Ghusaut Mula), Ram (Pali Mula), Raghav 
(Sakaradhi Mula), Chand (Sodarpur Mula), Harikar (Alay Mula), and Vidu 
(Tilay Mula). Despite their mothers' non-traditional backgrounds, their 
descendants were gradually integrated into the community over several 
generations of recorded marriages. 
Beyond social classification, the Dushana Panji functioned as an early system 
of medical genetics. The Panjikars systematically recorded families carrying 
hereditary physical and mental disorders, classifying them under specific 
medical categories : 
• 
Apasmari: Lineages with a history of epilepsy. 
• 
Unmadi: Lineages with a history of chronic mental illness or insanity. 
• 
Kunji: Lineages carrying physical deformities, such as blindness, 
deafness, or congenital lameness.
